title: The Macro Minute: Is the US-China trade dispute about to materially disrupt the Tech sector?

Happy Tuesday out there team 42 your
skipper here at Darius Dell to present
our macro minute for Tuesday June 10th
2025. As always we'll start with the
executive summary from today's leadup
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's key macro question is is the US
China trade dispute about to materially
disrupt the tech sector. Our answer
unlikely. In our view, the AI trade has
tangible upside from here, especially as
real e real economy growth slows amid
our U-shaped economy thesis. US China
trade talks enter day two in London
after day one of trade negotiations
yielded no breakthrough. In response to
the lack of progress, President Donald
Trump indicated China's not easy. US
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnik
confirmed talks will continue all day
Tuesday with a focus on reviving
Geneva's stalled agreement. Any missteps
in trade negotiations will translate to
elevated market risk due to tech market
leadership. And NASDAQ 100 is up 25%
from its April 7th low versus the S&P
500 up only 20%. The economic risk is
noteworthy, too, as evidenced by Taiwan
semiconductor posting a 40% revenue jump
in May on chip hoarding triggered by
trade fears. As we predicted, China
continues to run circles around the US
in trade negotiations. Step one, agree
to a daton. Step two, violate the terms
of the detent within weeks. Step three,
extract more concessions from a US
administration desperate to deliver wins
to anti- consumers, businesses, and
investors back home in subsequent
negotiations. Investors should expect
this pattern to continue because the
Trump administration cannot afford to
accelerate strategic decoupling from
China if their goal is to outrun
bipartisan fiscal large s from a debt to
GDP perspective. Elsewhere, there are
mounting signs that the AI super cycle
is accelerating sharply. The impact AI
will have on society is being grossly
underestimated by investor consensus. In
our view, investors that lazily point to
the development of technologies like the
cotton jan, automobile, and computer as
transformative tools that created jobs
on a net basis are missing the point.
Those physical technologies enabled
human beings to produce. AI replaces
human beings altogether. Why do we just
characterize the opposing view as
intellectually lazy, which is something
we rarely ever do? Because the data
already indicate it is wrong.
Specifically, the unemployment rate for
22 to 27 year olds with college degrees
hit 5.8% this spring, which is a 4-year
high amid hiring cuts and AI disruption
for entry-level white collar workers.
Anecdotal survey and hiring data
suggests this depressing trend will get
worse in the coming quarters. And before
we transition to our macro dashboard, I
just want to show a quick chart with you
from our friends over at the New York
Fed. Uh this one shows the unemployment
rates for recent college graduates
versus other groups. So the light blue
line is recent college graduates, uh age
22 to 27. The red line is all college
graduates, so 22 to, you know, anyone in
the labor force. And then the broader
unemployment rate for young workers, uh
uh 22 to 27, uh those without college uh
college degrees. And then the
unemployment rate for all workers is
here in this dark blue. As you can see
here, the light blue line, which is the
unemployment rate for recent college
graduates, has always been below the the
headline U3 unemployment rate. And it
started to kind of bump up against that,
you know, let's call it late uh in the
in in the as finally broke out above it
on a durable basis. And guess what
year? 2022. Do you know what happened in
2022?
I'll give you one guess. It starts with
a C and ends with a
GPT. Transitioning to our macro
dashboard to macro client dashboard
here. Uh as always, I'll wrap up with a
question from our community. This one's
regarding Fed chair and waiting says if
Trump decides on pile successor between
now and the end of the year, what weight
can we expect the Fed chair and waiting
say to have over monetary decisions? pal
would still have final decision over uh
final decision-making power or should we
expect him to cave to the expected
successor? Uh uh great question. Uh my
our take on I don't think we know the
answer. I don't think there there's no
data we can look at to determine the
answer for this. Uh but the uh our
expectation in terms of how this is
likely to play out is that if uh uh
President Trump names a Fed turning
waiting what would ultimately happen is
the bond market particularly the uh the
you know the the OIS market or sorry
money both money markets and the bond
market for any sort of duration that's
beyond pal's uh um tenor tenure as Fed
chair which ends in May of 2026 will
start to anchor on whatever the policy
guidance that's being provided for by
the Fed chair and waiting and
increasingly So, as you move through
time, like right now, let's say that's a
50/50 split. Maybe as you go forward a
couple of quarters, it'll be a 7525
splint in favor of whatever guidance the
uh uh the Fed chair and waiting is is um
is suggesting at the time. Um you know,
I think you have to assume that J Pal,
you know, in his last year at the helm
of the Federal Reserve, the guy who's in
his early to mid70s, this man's not
going to want to mess up a soft landing.
The last thing he wants to do is deliver
a soft landing and have it crumble right
before his very hand right in his very
hands and right before his very eyes
right before he uh gets out of the door.
He also doesn't want to let inflation
genie back out of the bottle again. So
he's doing what he has to do in terms of
maintaining stability and credibility uh
in the um in the bond market for the
time being which in my opinion I think
is actually uh aiding the the the the
business cycle in terms of not allowing
inflation to accelerate materially or
bond yields to jump condition higher in
a way that would force him to just start
hiking interest rates again. So he's
doing whatever he has to do to prevent
having to hike interest rates which you
know when you play the you know the kind
of the second and third order impacts of
that you know decision to be tough now
ultimately actually extends the duration
of the business cycle because they want
to have to be tougher later and so in my
opinion I think that's um I think as a
function of that and his desire to
engineer the soft landing and not let
the inflation genie out of the bottle
you're probably going to see a whole
heck of a lot of nothing uh but besides
you know kind of two-sided talk from Pal
which is what we've been seeing from Pal
in recent months And I would expect that
uh to continue. So in our view, Fed
chair and waiting is not going to have
much of an influence at all over
monetary policy decisions between now
and tenure the Fed as Fed chair being
done. But ultimately the market will
start to anchor on whatever the Fed
chair and waiting has to say with
regards to pricing in you know teners
that are what beyond pal's um pal's
expir expiration date at the Fed and
obviously into the bond market as well.
So just be aware of that. you might
start to see some different some split
pricing based on what the the the two um
the two men or I don't want to assume
it's going to be one man. I hope it's a
woman. Let's get a woman in there. How
about that? I would love to see a woman
as the next fetch here. Uh that'd be
great uh for society and for for our
country. So, I'm going to wrap it up
